reasoning: All four nail the core diagnosis (downstream exit block/boarding, balancing loop with delay, shifting-the-burden, induced demand), so discrimination comes from calibration, bias-awareness, and depth of verification. D wins: it gives the only explicit, well-split confidence (structural diagnosis high; exit-block-specific 70-75%, cheaply testable), names attribute substitution and the Senge swap test, supplies a clean falsifier section (what would change its mind), a denominator check decomposing ED LOS into segments, elective-surgery smoothing, and numbered Meadows leverage points; its only blemish is mild teleology ("the ED wants to survive"). C is a very close second with the tightest diagnostic checklist (four numbers with thresholds), sharp hard-to-vary/substitution reasoning, and the "surface the mental model as a testable claim" move, but it lacks D's explicit probability calibration. A is clean, robust-to-uncertainty, and has a well-structured Five-Whys verification plan, but is less deep and offers no quantified confidence. B has the strongest second-order-effects prose (burnout/retention spiral, mental-model hardening) but the thinnest verification rigor and vaguest confidence, placing it last.
